---
title: "Prediction Intervals"
---

A point prediction tells you the model's best guess; a prediction interval tells you how uncertain that guess is. For water quality decisions — permit compliance, flood warnings, sediment budget planning — knowing the uncertainty is often as important as knowing the predicted value.

This page uses **empirical conformal prediction intervals** (Mondrian approach), which provide a distribution-free coverage guarantee: if a 90% interval is reported, it actually contains 90% of observations on average. The method works by computing conformity scores (residuals) on LOGO cross-validation predictions, then stratifying by predicted SSC into 5 bins so that intervals widen naturally for higher concentrations. A continuous interpolation across 20 knot points produces smooth interval boundaries.

::: {.callout-note}
## Why not CQR?
We initially attempted Conformalized Quantile Regression (CQR) using a MultiQuantile CatBoost model trained for 23 hours. It failed: Box-Cox compression (lambda=0.2) prevents the Q95 quantile from reaching extreme SSC values, causing conditional coverage collapse at high concentrations. Empirical conformal intervals using point prediction residuals achieve better calibrated coverage without requiring a separate quantile model.
:::

## Coverage by Predicted SSC Bin

The core diagnostic: does the stated coverage match actual coverage? Each bar shows what fraction of holdout observations actually fell within the interval, broken out by predicted SSC bin. Bars near the dashed target line (90% or 80%) indicate proper calibration. The 2000+ mg/L bin has only 31 holdout samples, so its coverage estimate is noisy — this is the regime where the model struggles most and where interval width matters most for safety decisions.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-conformal-coverage
from pathlib import Path
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, ".")
from _scripts.figures import make_conformal_coverage_bars

fig = make_conformal_coverage_bars(Path("dashboard_data/conformal_conformal_holdout_coverage.json"))
fig.show()
```

## Interval Width vs Predicted SSC

How wide are the intervals at different concentration levels? For the intervals to be useful, their width should scale roughly with the predicted value — a 10 mg/L interval around a 20 mg/L prediction is informative, but the same width around a 2000 mg/L prediction is meaningless. This plot shows the 90% and 80% interval widths at 20 interpolation knots across the predicted SSC range. The dashed line marks where interval width equals predicted SSC — points below it mean the interval is narrower than the prediction itself (useful); points above mean the uncertainty exceeds the signal (less useful).

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-conformal-widths
from _scripts.figures import make_conformal_interval_widths

fig = make_conformal_interval_widths(Path("dashboard_data/conformal_conformal_summary.json"))
fig.show()
```

## Prediction Interval Fan Plot

Point predictions are most misleading during storm events that can spike SSC by two orders of magnitude in hours. A well-calibrated uncertainty model should widen its intervals when conditions are extreme. This fan plot shows time series for three holdout sites with the most data, with the 90% prediction band shaded. The intervals are computed by interpolating the empirical conformal offsets at each predicted SSC value. Narrow bands that stay narrow through a storm peak would be a warning sign — the empirical approach avoids this by construction, since offsets are calibrated on actual residual magnitudes.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-conformal-fan-plot
from _scripts.figures import make_conformal_fan_plot

fig = make_conformal_fan_plot(
    Path("dashboard_data/per_reading.parquet"),
    Path("dashboard_data/conformal_conformal_summary.json"),
)
fig.show()
```
